Nietzsche → Rand → e/acc: The 150-Year Arc

2 min read · 430 words · Deep Dive #103 · 2026-04-09
Source: Thus Spoke Zarathustra (1883) / Atlas Shrugged (1957) / e/acc movement (2022+)

Three moments. Three manifestos. One argument that keeps coming back because we keep forgetting it.

In 1883, Nietzsche drew the line. Humanity has two trajectories: the Übermensch — the self-overcoming individual who creates new values and accepts the vertigo of greatness — or the Last Man, who blinks, invents comfort, and calls it happiness. Zarathustra didn't present this as a debate. He presented it as a choice with a deadline. Choose the Übermensch or the species stagnates. There is no stable middle ground.

In 1957, Rand took Nietzsche's abstraction and built a 1,168-page simulation of what happens when the Last Men win. Atlas Shrugged isn't just philosophy — it's a systems model. When you punish producers for producing, regulate innovators into submission, and redistribute not wealth but ambition, the motor stops. Not with a bang. With a shrug. The creators don't fight. They leave. And the world discovers, too late, what was holding it up.

In 2022, e/acc emerged as the tech world's version of the same argument. Beff Jezos and the accelerationist movement said what Nietzsche and Rand said, but in the language of thermodynamics and information theory: civilization is a entropy-reducing machine, and anything that slows it down — overregulation, safety theater, institutional risk-aversion — is a civilizational threat. The d/acc counter (Vitalik's defensive accelerationism) argues for acceleration with guardrails.

The remarkable thing is how little the core tension has moved in 150 years. The vocabulary changes — will to power, rational self-interest, thermodynamic optimization — but the fault line stays the same: do we accept the risks of excellence, or do we protect ourselves into irrelevance?

Nietzsche warned that the Last Man's happiness is a trap. Rand showed the trap closing. e/acc is arguing about whether to spring it open. Each generation rediscovers that safety and greatness are not compatible goals — and that choosing safety feels like wisdom right up until the moment it becomes stagnation.

The 150-year arc isn't a coincidence. It's the same argument because it's the same species, facing the same fork, and — most of the time — making the same comfortable choice. The crowd still wants the Last Man. The question is whether the builders keep building anyway.
